Brigade.title: Brigade
Brigade.description: Represents a brigade, the highest level unit managing templates and overall readiness.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Brigade entity.

Battalion.title: Battalion
Battalion.description: Represents a battalion, reporting readiness status to a brigade.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Battalion entity.

Template.title: Template
Template.description: Defines the required roles, authorizations, and equipment for a specific unit type.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Template entity.

Role.title: Role
Role.description: Represents a role defined within a template.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Role entity.

Personnel.title: Personnel
Personnel.description: Represents a member of the personnel.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Personnel entity.

GapAnalysis.title: GapAnalysis
GapAnalysis.description: Represents the discrepancies between the template and actual status.

id.description: Unique identifier for the GapAnalysis entity.

analysisDate.description: Date the gap analysis was performed.

definition.description: Represents a brigade.  The root level for organizing templates and battalions.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure is designed to support the TroopSync application's core features, focusing on template management, automated gap analysis, battalion reporting, role assignment, and user authentication/authorization. The structure prioritizes authorization independence by denormalizing authorization-related data.  The design employs structural segregation to ensure homogeneous security postures within collections.  Access modeling leverages path-based ownership and membership maps for collaborative data. 

To ensure Authorization Independence (Strategy A), key authorization data, such as the brigadeId, is denormalized into subcollections where access depends on it. This avoids the need for `get()` calls in security rules, enabling atomic operations.

Structural Segregation (Strategy B) is enforced by separating data with different access requirements into distinct collections.  For example, brigade-owned data (templates) are stored separately from battalion-specific data (gap analyses and personnel). This simplifies the security rules.

Access Modeling (Strategy C) utilizes hierarchical paths for user-owned data (e.g., `/brigades/{brigadeId}/templates/{templateId}`) and standardizes on membership maps where appropriate.  Path-based ownership enforces strict ownership, and role-based access control (DBAC) can be implemented with a dedicated `/roles` collection.

The data structure supports the required QAPs by using segregation and path-based ownership for secure list operations. For example, listing templates is restricted to brigade members via the `brigades/{brigadeId}/templates` collection. Similarly, battalion-specific data is secured under the `/brigades/{brigadeId}/battalions/{battalionId}` path.
